title: Voltage regulator/rectifier failure — all eras
description: The voltage regulator/rectifier is the single most common electrical failure on Harleys across every generation. It converts AC from the stator to DC, and regulates voltage to ~14V. When it fails, the battery either overcharges (boiling acid, swelling) or undercharges (dead battery, dim lights). The part runs hot and is often mounted in a low-airflow area.
fix_procedure: 1. Test: engine running at 2000 RPM, voltmeter across battery terminals — should read 13.5-14.5V. Below 13V = not charging. Above 15V = overcharging. 2. If not charging, check stator output first (AC volts at stator connector, engine running — should see 30-50 VAC per leg). 3. If stator is good, regulator is bad. 4. Replace with MOSFET-type regulator — they run cooler and last longer than OEM shunt-type. 5. Forum tip: the #1 Harley electrical upgrade is a MOSFET regulator. The OEM shunt-type regulators dump excess energy as heat — they're literally designed to cook themselves. A MOSFET regulator (Cycle Electric, Rick's Motorsport) runs cool to the touch and lasts 100K+ miles. Worth the $80 upgrade on ANY Harley. Also, relocate the regulator to a high-airflow area if possible — behind the engine is the worst place, but that's where Harley puts them.
parts_needed: MOSFET voltage regulator (Cycle Electric or Rick's)

title: Stator failure — burn and short
description: The stator (AC generator) windings burn and short-circuit from heat, oil contamination, or insulation breakdown. Symptoms: battery goes dead, lights dim at idle, burning smell from primary/engine area. The stator is inside the primary cover on most Harleys — removing it requires draining primary fluid.
causes: Stator connector melt increasing resistance and heat
Regulator failure causing stator to work harder
fix_procedure: 1. Stator test: disconnect stator connector. Ohmmeter across each pair of stator leads — should read 0.1-0.5 ohms (check manual for exact spec). Infinite = open winding. 2. Test each lead to ground — should read infinite (open). Any reading = shorted to ground. 3. AC voltage test: engine running, measure AC across stator leads — all three pairs should be within 10% of each other. 4. If failed, replace stator. 5. ALWAYS replace the regulator when replacing the stator — a bad regulator killed the stator in the first place, and will kill the new one too. 6. Forum tip: when you pull the primary cover to replace a stator, inspect the stator connector — if it's melted or discolored, hard-wire with ring terminals. The stator connector is the #2 cause of charging failures after the regulator. Cycle Electric makes stator + regulator kits for every Harley generation — buy the kit, not just the stator.
parts_needed: Stator (Cycle Electric kit with regulator recommended)
Stator connector (or ring terminals for hard-wire)

title: Starter solenoid failure — clicks but won't crank
description: The starter solenoid (relay) is the most common 'click but no crank' culprit on Harleys. The high-current contacts inside the solenoid pit and corrode over time, eventually failing to pass enough current to spin the starter motor. You hear a strong click from the solenoid but the starter doesn't engage.
causes: Solenoid contact pitting from high current
Weak battery increasing current draw through solenoid
fix_procedure: 1. Test: key on, hit start button. Strong click but no crank = solenoid contacts. Rapid clicking = weak battery. No click = solenoid coil, start button, or clutch/neutral safety switch. 2. Jump test: bridge the two large terminals on the solenoid with a screwdriver (BIKE IN NEUTRAL, stand clear). If starter spins, solenoid contacts are bad. 3. Replace solenoid — it's a $20-40 part. 4. While there, clean and tighten ALL battery cable connections (positive, negative, ground to frame). 5. Forum tip: 90% of 'won't start' calls at the shop are battery or solenoid — not the starter motor. Always check the simple stuff first. The HD solenoid is under the seat or on the starter motor depending on the year. Carry a spare — they're small and cheap. Also, the battery-to-solenoid cable is often corroded inside the insulation where you can't see it — flex it and look for green/white corrosion at the terminals.

title: Ground cable corrosion — mysterious electrical gremlins
description: Bad ground connections cause more unexplained electrical problems on Harleys than any other single cause. Dim lights, flickering gauges, intermittent no-start, weird turn signal behavior, ABS lights — all can be traced to a single corroded ground cable or ground point on the frame.
causes: Engine-to-frame ground strap broken or missing
Paint or powder coat insulating ground points
fix_procedure: 1. Start at the battery negative terminal — remove, clean to bright metal, reinstall with dielectric grease. 2. Trace the ground cable to the frame — remove the bolt, clean the frame contact area to bare metal, reinstall. 3. Check the engine-to-frame ground strap (usually near the front motor mount) — clean both ends. 4. On 2011+ CAN bus bikes, there are additional ground points for the ECM and BCM — check those too. 5. Forum tip: the single best preventive maintenance you can do on a Harley's electrical system is cleaning grounds once a year. Every mysterious electrical problem I've ever chased on a Harley started with a bad ground. Buy a wire brush attachment for your drill and hit every ground point. Add dielectric grease after to prevent future corrosion. Also, add a supplemental ground strap from the battery negative directly to the engine case — the factory ground path goes through the frame, which corrodes.

title: CAN bus communication fault — 2011+ models
description: 2011+ Harleys use CAN bus (Controller Area Network) for communication between the ECM, BCM, TSSM, instrument cluster, ABS module, and other nodes. A CAN bus fault can cause multiple unrelated warning lights, limp mode, no-start, or total electrical shutdown. Water intrusion, connector corrosion, or a single damaged wire can take down the entire bus.
causes: Water intrusion into CAN bus connectors
Damaged CAN bus wire (chafed against frame)
Failed CAN bus node (ECM, BCM, TSSM, ABS module)
fix_procedure: 1. Scan for U-codes (communication fault codes) — they tell you which module lost communication. 2. U0100 = ECM lost, U0121 = ABS lost, U0155 = instrument cluster lost. 3. Check the CAN bus backbone connectors — they're usually under the seat or behind the side covers. Look for green corrosion or water. 4. If a specific module is offline, check its power and ground first, then the CAN H and CAN L wires at its connector. 5. CAN bus wires are twisted pair — check for continuity and correct resistance (60 ohms between CAN H and CAN L at the diagnostic port). 6. Forum tip: CAN bus problems are terrifying because everything goes haywire at once, but the fix is usually a single corroded connector. Start at the diagnostic port under the seat — measure 60 ohms between pins 6 and 14. If you see 120 ohms, one of the two terminating resistors is disconnected (a module fell off the bus). If you see 0 ohms, CAN H and CAN L are shorted. A $30 CAN bus reader from Amazon can tell you which node is down without a dealer scan tool.

title: Ignition switch failure — intermittent power loss
description: The ignition switch on Harleys develops internal contact wear that causes intermittent total power loss. The bike dies mid-ride, instruments go black, and may or may not restart immediately. On older models it's the mechanical switch; on newer models it's the electronic FOB/key system (TSSM).
causes: Key cylinder worn (excessive play allows intermittent contact)
Ignition switch connector looseness from vibration
fix_procedure: 1. If bike dies intermittently and ALL power is lost (not just engine — lights, gauges, everything), it's the ignition switch or main circuit breaker. 2. Wiggle the key while running — if the bike cuts out or flickers, the switch is bad. 3. Check the ignition switch connector (under the dash/tank) for looseness. 4. On 2004+ models with security: if the security light flashes and the bike won't start, the TSSM isn't recognizing the FOB. Try the second FOB. 5. Replace the ignition switch or TSSM antenna as needed. 6. Forum tip: before replacing the ignition switch ($100+), try cleaning the connector and applying dielectric grease. Also check the main 40A circuit breaker (usually under the seat) — a loose breaker mimics the same symptoms as a bad ignition switch. On pre-2004 Harleys, the ignition switch is a $30 part and takes 15 minutes to replace — just do it if it's suspect.

title: Turn signal module (TSM/TSSM) malfunction
description: The Turn Signal Module (TSM) or Turn Signal Security Module (TSSM) controls turn signals, hazards, auto-cancel, and (on TSSM-equipped bikes) the security system. When it fails, you get stuck turn signals, no signals at all, security lockout, or an alarm that goes off randomly.
fix_procedure: 1. If no turn signals: check fuses first, then test bulbs. If fuses and bulbs are good, TSM is suspect. 2. If security lockout (red light flashing, won't start): try second FOB. If no FOBs work, the TSSM antenna ring may be misaligned — it wraps around the ignition switch. 3. If alarm goes off randomly: check the TSSM motion sensor sensitivity — it's adjustable on some models. 4. Battery disconnect for 10 minutes can reset the TSSM. 5. If TSSM is dead, dealer programming is required for the replacement (FOBs must be paired). 6. Forum tip: the TSSM is the single most annoying part on a modern Harley. If it fails, you need a dealer scan tool to reprogram the FOBs to a new unit — you can't DIY this. Some guys eliminate the security system entirely by installing a non-security TSM from a base model, but you lose keyless features. The #1 cause of random security lockout is a dead FOB battery — replace it yearly ($3 CR2032 or CR2016).

title: Wiring harness chafing — heat and vibration damage
description: Harley wiring harnesses chafe against the frame, engine, and each other from vibration. The V-twin vibration is particularly destructive to wiring over time. Chafed insulation causes shorts, intermittent faults, and potentially fires if a hot wire contacts fuel or oil.
causes: Wiring routed too close to exhaust
Zip ties cutting into wire insulation over time
fix_procedure: 1. Visual inspection: trace the main harness from headlight through frame backbone to tail. Look for: bare wire showing through insulation, melted insulation near exhaust, zip tie cuts, harness rubbing against frame edges. 2. Pay special attention to: under tank (hot engine heat rises), through frame backbone (vibration), and at the steering head (flexing on turns). 3. Repair: solder and heat-shrink individual wires, or replace harness sections. 4. Re-route any wires touching exhaust. 5. Use split-loom or textile wrap on repaired sections. 6. Forum tip: check under the tank every oil change — that's where 80% of Harley wiring damage occurs because heat rises from the engine directly into the harness. Also, NEVER use zip ties tight against wiring — use adhesive-backed tie-down mounts so the zip tie holds the bundle away from metal edges. Electrical tape is not a permanent repair — always use heat-shrink or split-loom.

title: Battery type mismatch — wrong charger or wrong battery
description: Harleys have used conventional lead-acid, AGM, and now lithium batteries across different eras. Using the wrong charger type or installing the wrong battery type causes premature battery death, overcharging, undercharging, or failure to start in cold weather.
causes: AGM battery charged with conventional charger (overcharge)
Lithium battery on AGM charger (undercharge or damage)
Battery too small for engine compression (CCA too low)
fix_procedure: 1. Identify your battery type: conventional (has caps), AGM (sealed, usually Harley OEM since ~2007), or lithium (lightweight, aftermarket). 2. Match your charger: AGM needs an AGM-specific tender (Battery Tender, CTEK), lithium needs a lithium-specific mode. 3. For cold-climate Harleys, AGM is more reliable than lithium — lithium batteries lose significant CCA below 40F. 4. Minimum CCA for Harley: Sportster 200+, Big Twin 300+, M8 Touring 400+. 5. Forum tip: the best overall battery for any Harley is a quality AGM (Yuasa, Deka/East Penn). Lithium saves 5-10 lbs but if you're in a cold climate or the bike sits, it's not worth the risk. The Harley OEM battery is a rebranded Deka — it's actually good. Charger recommendation: CTEK CT5 Power Sport — it has AGM and lithium modes, maintains without overcharging, and works in the garage 24/7. Battery Tender Jr is fine too but doesn't have lithium mode.
parts_needed: Battery (AGM: Yuasa or Deka, or Lithium: Shorai/Antigravity)
Smart charger/tender (CTEK CT5 or Battery Tender)

title: Headlight and lighting circuit overload
description: Adding aftermarket lighting (LED bars, auxiliary lights, heated grips, USB chargers) without upgrading the wiring or adding relays overloads the factory lighting circuit. This blows fuses, melts connectors, dims the headlight, and on older bikes can overload the stator/regulator system.
causes: Total accessory load exceeding circuit capacity
Accessories wired to lighting circuit instead of battery direct
Stator output exceeded by total electrical load
fix_procedure: 1. Calculate total electrical load: add up all accessories in watts. Compare to stator output (Evo ~300W, TC96 ~400W, M8 ~450W, Rev Max ~500W). 2. If total load > 80% of stator output, you'll have charging problems at idle. 3. Wire high-draw accessories (heated grips, lights >30W) directly to battery through a relay — not to existing circuits. 4. Use an inline fuse on every accessory circuit. 5. Consider upgrading to a higher-output stator if total load is consistently high. 6. Forum tip: the #1 mistake I see is wiring heated grips to the lighting circuit. Heated grips draw 30-50W — that's the entire headlight budget. Wire them through a relay to the battery. Same for any LED light bar. The rule of thumb: anything over 15W gets its own fused relay circuit. A Harley at idle with the headlight, heated grips, GPS, and phone charger running can exceed the charging system output — the battery fills the gap, but only for so long.
